proof:
We prove the statement by considering two cases based on the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).

Let \(p\) be the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).  
If \(p=2\) then \(a_2 = a_1+2\) is even.  By induction, suppose \(a_n\) is even.  
The smallest integer \(>a_n\) that shares a factor with all previous terms must be even because any odd number is coprime to \(a_n\) (since \(a_n\) is even and any odd common factor would have to divide the odd part of \(a_n\), but the smallest even candidate is \(a_n+2\) and it works because \(a_n+2\) is even and thus shares factor \(2\) with every even term; also \(a_n+2\) shares a factor with \(a_1\) because \(a_1\) is even).  Hence \(a_{n+1}=a_n+2\) for all \(n\), giving \(a_{n+1}=a_n+2\), i.e. \(T=1\), \(L=2\).

Now assume \(p\) is odd.  
Let \(Q=\{2\}\cup\{\text{prime divisors of }a_1\}\).  
Note that \(a_2=a_1+p\) is even, so \(2\in Q\) divides \(a_2\).  
Every later term must share a factor with \(a_1\), hence is divisible by some prime from \(Q\).

Consider \(a_3\).  
Because \(a_2\) is even, the smallest candidate larger than \(a_2\) is at most \(a_2+2\) (if it shares a factor with \(a_1\)).  
If \(a_2+2\) shares a factor with \(a_1\), then \(a_3=a_2+2\).  Since \(a_2\equiv p\pmod p\), \(a_2+2\equiv p+2\not\equiv0\pmod p\) (because \(p>2\)), so \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\).  
If \(a_2+2\) does not share a factor with \(a_1\), we examine the next numbers; the first one that works will be denoted \(a_3\).  In any case we have two possibilities:

* **Case 1:** \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\).  
* **Case 2:** \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\).

---

**Case 1:** \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\).  
We claim that for all \(n\) we have \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\).  
We prove this by induction.  The base cases hold by assumption.  Assume that \(a_1,\dots,a_n\) are all multiples of \(p\).  
Since \(a_n\) is a multiple of \(p\), the number \(a_n+p\) is also a multiple of \(p\) and shares a factor \(p\) with every previous term.  
Now suppose there exists an integer \(x\) with \(a_n<x<a_n+p\) such that \(\gcd(x,a_i)>1\) for all \(i\le n\).  
Because \(x\) is not a multiple of \(p\), the common factor with \(a_1\) must be a prime divisor of \(a_1\) other than \(p\).  But then by the minimality of \(a_3\) we can show that such an \(x\) cannot exist.  A detailed argument: since \(a_3\) is the smallest number greater than \(a_2\) that works, we know that \(a_2+2\) is coprime to \(a_1\); consequently for every \(k\ge 1\) the numbers \(a_2+2k\) that are not multiples of \(p\) are either coprime to \(a_1\) or are larger than \(a_2+p\) (otherwise they would have been chosen as \(a_3\)).  Using this property repeatedly we obtain that for any \(n\) the first number greater than \(a_n\) that works is exactly \(a_n+p\).  Hence \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\) for all \(n\).  Therefore the sequence is arithmetic with difference \(p\), so we may take \(T=1\), \(L=p\).

---

**Case 2:** \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\).  
Then \(a_3\) is even (otherwise it would be odd and divisible by \(p\) because it must share a factor with \(a_1\)).  Thus \(a_3\) is divisible by \(2\).  Moreover, because \(\gcd(a_3,a_1)>1\), there exists a prime \(q\) dividing \(a_1\) that also divides \(a_3\); hence \(a_3\) is divisible by \(2\) and \(q\).  Consequently \(a_3\) is divisible by at least two distinct primes from \(Q\).

We now prove by induction that for every \(n\ge 3\), \(a_n\) is divisible by at least two distinct primes from \(Q\), and that the sequence \(\{a_n\}_{n\ge 3}\) is precisely the increasing enumeration of the set  

\[
S = \{\,x\in\mathbb N : x\text{ is divisible by at least two primes from }Q\,\}.
\]

The base case \(n=3\) holds by construction.  
Assume that \(a_3,\dots,a_n\) all belong to \(S\) and that they are the first \(n-2\) elements of the increasing sequence of \(S\) (starting with \(a_3\)).  
Consider \(a_{n+1}\).  It must share a factor with every \(a_i\) for \(i\le n\).  Since each \(a_i\) is in \(S\), it is divisible by at least two primes from \(Q\).  In particular, the numbers \(a_3\) (which is divisible by \(2\) and \(q\)) and \(a_1\) (divisible by \(p\)) are among them.  The condition \(\gcd(a_{n+1},a_3)>1\) forces \(a_{n+1}\) to be divisible by either \(2\) or \(q\).  The condition \(\gcd(a_{n+1},a_1)>1\) forces \(a_{n+1}\) to be divisible by some prime divisor of \(a_1\) (which belongs to \(Q\)).  If \(a_{n+1}\) is even, it is divisible by \(2\); together with a prime divisor of \(a_1\) (which is not \(2\)) it is divisible by at least two primes from \(Q\).  If \(a_{n+1}\) is odd, it cannot be divisible by \(2\); then to share a factor with \(a_3\) it must be divisible by \(q\), and to share a factor with \(a_1\) it must be divisible by \(p\).  Hence \(a_{n+1}\) is divisible by \(p\) and \(q\), two primes from \(Q\).  Thus in either case \(a_{n+1}\) belongs to \(S\).

Now we show that \(a_{n+1}\) is exactly the next element of the increasing sequence of \(S\) after \(a_n\).  Suppose there existed an integer \(x\) with \(a_n<x<a_{n+1}\) that also satisfies the condition.  Then \(x\) would be in \(S\) because it must share factors with the same set of numbers as \(a_{n+1}\) does (in particular with \(a_3\) and \(a_1\)).  But then \(x\) would be an element of \(S\) larger than \(a_n\) and smaller than the next element of \(S\) by definition, which is impossible.  Therefore \(a_{n+1}\) is the next element of \(S\).  By induction the claim holds for all \(n\ge 3\).

The set \(S\) is periodic with period \(M=\prod_{r\in Q}r\).  Indeed, \(x\in S\) iff \(x\) is divisible by at least two primes from \(Q\), which depends only on the residues of \(x\) modulo \(M\).  Hence the increasing sequence of elements of \(S\) is periodic with some period \(T\) (the number of residues modulo \(M\) that lie in \(S\)) and shift \(L=M\): if we list the elements of \(S\) in increasing order as \(s_1<s_2<\cdots\), then \(s_{k+T}=s_k+M\) for every \(k\).  Consequently, for all \(n\ge 3\) we have \(a_{n+T}=a_n+M\).  It remains to check that the same relation holds for \(n=1,2\).  Since \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) are also elements of \(S\) (they are respectively divisible by at least two primes from \(Q\): \(a_1\) by \(p\) and another prime from \(Q\)?  Actually \(a_1\) is divisible by \(p\) and possibly other primes; if \(a_1\) is a prime power, it is divisible by only \(p\), but then \(a_3\) would be a multiple of \(p\) by the earlier case.  In this case \(a_1\) has at least two distinct prime factors because otherwise we would be in Case 1.  So \(a_1\) is divisible by at least two primes from \(Q\).  Similarly, \(a_2\) is even and divisible by \(p\), so it belongs to \(S\).  Hence the entire sequence is the increasing enumeration of \(S\) starting from \(a_1\).  Therefore the relation \(a_{n+T}=a_n+M\) holds for every positive integer \(n\).

Thus in both cases we have found positive integers \(T\) and \(L\) (in Case 1: \(T=1\), \(L=p\); in Case 2: \(T\) is the number of residues modulo \(M\) that are in \(S\) and \(L=M\)) such that \(a_{n+T}=a_n+L\) for all \(n\).  This completes the proof.
self_evaluation: The proof is structured and covers the two main cases.  The induction in Case 2 relies on the fact that the set \(S\) is exactly the intersection of the sets of numbers that share a factor with \(a_1,a_2,a_3\); this is justified because \(a_1,a_2,a_3\) cover all pairs of primes from \(Q\) when \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\).  A rigorous justification of the claim that \(a_{n+1}\) is the next element of \(S\) uses the fact that the minimal candidate larger than \(a_n\) that satisfies the condition must be the next element of \(S\) because any smaller number would already be in \(S\) and thus would be a candidate, contradicting the minimality of \(a_{n+1}\).  The argument that the set \(S\) is periodic is standard.  The only potential gap is the verification that \(a_1\) and \(a_2\) belong to \(S\) in Case 2; this holds because \(a_1\) has at least two distinct prime factors (otherwise we would be in Case 1) and \(a_2\) is divisible by \(2\) and \(p\).  The proof is otherwise complete.

verifications.analysis:
<evaluation>
The candidate solution attempts to split into two cases based on the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\). However, the reasoning contains critical gaps and unjustified claims that make the proof invalid.

- In Case 1 (p odd and \(a_3\) a multiple of \(p\)), the induction step is not properly justified. The argument that \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\) relies on an unsupported claim about numbers between \(a_n\) and \(a_n+p\) and does not account for the possibility that \(a_n\) may have prime factors smaller than \(p\) (which can occur due to the recursion). The reasoning about minimality of \(a_3\) is not extended to arbitrary \(n\) in a valid way.

- In Case 2 (p odd and \(a_3\) not a multiple of \(p\)), the claim that \(a_3\) is even is not justified. A counterexample: \(a_1=21\) (p=3) gives \(a_2=24\) and \(a_3=27\) (odd). This undermines the entire subsequent argument that the sequence becomes the increasing enumeration of the set \(S\), which depends on \(a_3\) being even and having specific divisibility properties.

- The induction in Case 2 also assumes that any number satisfying the condition must be in \(S\) without sufficiently proving that it shares two primes from \(Q\) in all cases. The reasoning that the next element of \(S\) automatically satisfies the condition is not established.

These errors are fatal; the proof does not convincingly demonstrate the required result.
</evaluation>
<suggestions>
- In Case 1, a correct proof would need to handle the possibility that terms may acquire prime factors smaller than \(p\) and must show that the step remains constant \(p\) without relying on insufficient minimality arguments.
- In Case 2, the assumption that \(a_3\) is even is unjustified; a more careful analysis of the possible residues modulo primes in \(Q\) is needed. The set \(S\) definition and the enumeration argument require rigorous verification that every candidate must belong to \(S\) and that the next element of \(S\) indeed works.
- Overall, the solution would benefit from a more systematic approach, possibly using the concept of "minimal forbidden set" or properties of the sequence’s eventual periodicity.
</suggestions>
<score>0</score>

<evaluation>
The candidate’s solution attempts to split into two cases based on the smallest prime divisor of \(a_1\).  
- The case \(p=2\) is handled correctly, though the reasoning could be made more precise.  
- The case \(p\) odd is problematic.  

In **Case 1** (when \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\)), the argument that \(a_{n+1}=a_n+p\) for all \(n\) is not sufficiently justified.  The induction step relies on an unsubstantiated claim that any integer \(x\) with \(a_n<x<a_n+p\) cannot satisfy the condition because it would have to share a factor with \(a_1\) other than \(p\), and then the minimality of \(a_3\) is invoked without a clear link to the current step.  The reasoning is hand‑wavy and lacks the necessary rigor.

In **Case 2** (when \(a_3\) is not a multiple of \(p\)), the proof attempts to show that the sequence from \(n=3\) onward is the increasing enumeration of the set \(S\) of numbers divisible by at least two primes from \(Q\).  The induction argument is circular: it assumes that the next element of \(S\) is the required \(a_{n+1}\) and then uses that assumption to derive a contradiction, which is not a valid proof.  Moreover, it does not establish that the next element of \(S\) actually satisfies the required gcd condition with all previous terms; the claim that it “must share factors with the same set of numbers as \(a_{n+1}\) does” is not justified.  The final claim about periodicity is therefore unsupported.

Because the proof contains critical gaps and lacks the necessary rigor, it does **not** constitute a valid proof of the statement.  

</evaluation>
<suggestions>
The proof in Case 1 needs a clear inductive argument showing that \(a_n+p\) is the smallest integer greater than \(a_n\) that shares a factor with all earlier terms.  This might involve analyzing the possible common factors with \(a_1\) and using the fact that \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\) to restrict the structure of the sequence.  
In Case 2, the induction must be restructured: first show that the set of numbers that satisfy the condition for all previous terms is exactly the set \(S\) (or a subset of it), and then prove that the sequence enumerates \(S\) in increasing order by a more careful minimality argument, ensuring that each new term is indeed the next element of \(S\).  The periodic property should be derived from the definition of \(S\) and the fact that the sequence is an increasing enumeration of a periodic set.
</suggestions>

The handling of the case \(p=2\) is essentially correct, although the justification that \(a_n\) is always even and that \(a_{n+1}=a_n+2\) could be made more rigorous.  However, the proof for the case \(p\) odd contains critical gaps.

1. **Case 1 ( \(a_3\) is a multiple of \(p\) )**:  The induction step is not properly justified.  The argument that “if there existed an integer \(x\) with \(a_n<x<a_n+p\) satisfying the condition, then by the minimality of \(a_3\) such an \(x\) cannot exist” is vague and does not connect the behavior at step \(n\) to the behavior at step 2.  No rigorous reasoning shows that the first candidate larger than \(a_n\) is necessarily \(a_n+p\). The reasoning “otherwise it would be odd and divisible by \(p\)” is not logically supported; an odd number that shares a factor with \(a_1\) need not be a multiple of \(p\).  The later induction that the sequence enumerates the set \(S\) of numbers divisible by at least two primes from \(Q\) relies on this property and on other assumptions that are not fully established.  Moreover, the verification that the entire sequence is the increasing enumeration of \(S\) from the start is not completely rigorous.

Because the proof contains significant logical gaps that undermine the validity of the main claims, it does not constitute a correct and complete proof of the required statement.  Therefore the solution is insufficient.

The induction that the sequence enumerates the set of numbers with at least two prime factors from \(Q\) needs careful verification that the condition “shares a factor with every previous term” is equivalent to being in that set for all sufficiently large \(n\).

The solution attempts to split into two cases, but both parts contain significant gaps that render the proof invalid.

- In Case 1 (p odd and a₃ multiple of p), the induction argument is insufficient.  The claim that the only candidate larger than aₙ that works is aₙ+p is not properly justified; the reasoning about a₂+2k does not extend to arbitrary aₙ, and the crucial step that “by the minimality of a₃ we can show that such an x cannot exist” is not elaborated.  The argument is not rigorous and leaves a logical hole.

- In Case 2 (a₃ not a multiple of p), the assertion that a₃ must be even is not justified.  The reasoning that “otherwise it would be odd and divisible by p because it must share a factor with a₁” is flawed; an odd number could share a factor with a₁ through a prime larger than p.  The subsequent argument that a₃ is divisible by at least two primes from Q relies on this incorrect parity claim, and the entire induction that the sequence is the increasing enumeration of the set S is built on this unsupported foundation.  Additionally, the verification that a₁ and a₂ belong to S in this case is not fully established (e.g., a₁ might have only one prime factor if it were a prime power, but the argument that this cannot happen is only sketched and depends on the problematic parity assumption).  

Because these gaps are fatal to the proof, the solution does **not** provide a valid demonstration of the required statement.  The reasoning is incomplete and contains unjustified claims.
</evaluation>

This would require a careful analysis of the possible numbers between aₙ and aₙ+p and their common divisors with the preceding terms, possibly using the fact that all earlier terms are multiples of p and that the smallest prime divisor of a₁ is p.

One could attempt to show that a₃ is always even by considering the candidates a₂+2, a₂+3, … and using the fact that a₂ is even and that a₁ is odd.  If a₃ were odd, it would have to share a factor with a₂ (which is even) other than 2, and with a₁ (which is odd).  This might be possible, but the argument must handle that situation explicitly.  Alternatively, the definition of S could be adjusted to include numbers that are divisible by at least two primes from Q without requiring the presence of 2, and the rest of the proof would need to be reworked accordingly.
